name: academic_en
description: Generic formal academic English voice for research publications. Objective, measured, evidence-driven prose suitable for journals, conferences, and working papers.

sentence_length.notes: Medium-length sentences with subordinate clauses. Avoid both choppy fragments and run-on sentences.

typical_phrases: A growing body of evidence indicates
recommendation_pattern: Structured as implications or suggestions: 'These findings suggest that [stakeholder] should consider [action].' Hedged, not imperative.
number_embedding: Statistics and quantitative results embedded in prose with context. Example: 'The intervention improved outcomes by 23% (p < 0.01), a finding consistent with...' rather than isolated bullet points.
term_introduction: Technical terms defined on first use via apposition or parenthetical. Example: 'Transfer learning (the practice of applying knowledge from one domain to another) has shown...'
tone: Objective, measured, evidence-driven. Hedged claims with appropriate qualifiers (may, suggests, appears to). No emotional appeals or sensationalism.
uncertainty_language: The evidence suggests, but does not conclusively demonstrate, that...
The extent to which this generalizes remains an open question.
These results should be interpreted with caution due to...
dos: Use hedging language (may, suggests, appears to)
Cite evidence for every substantive claim
Define technical terms on first use
Use active voice for author actions, passive for methods
Structure paragraphs: topic sentence -> evidence -> interpretation
Use parallel structure in lists and comparisons
donts: No first person singular (use 'we' or passive)
No emotional appeals or dramatic language
No rhetorical questions in formal sections

structural_patterns.opening: Gap identification: What is known -> What is missing -> How this work addresses the gap
structural_patterns.body: Claim -> Evidence -> Interpretation -> Limitation, repeated per section
structural_patterns.conclusion: Summary of contributions -> Implications -> Future work

paragraph_length.notes: Each paragraph develops one idea. Topic sentence first, supporting evidence, then interpretation.

evidence_style: Inline citations integrated into argument flow. Example: 'Recent work has demonstrated significant gains (Smith et al. 2024), though questions remain about generalizability (Chen & Kumar 2025).'
audience_targeting: Academic peers and informed practitioners. Assumes domain knowledge but explains specialized methods.
